standard_description: Use relative pronouns (who, whose, whom, which, that) and relative adverbs (where, when, why).
subject: Language

khan_exercises.title: Relative pronouns
khan_exercises.description: Learn to use relative pronouns to link clauses together!

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct relative pronoun!**

>The chocolates  \_\_\_\_\_\_  you gave me were fantastic.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Relative pronouns allow us to add descriptive phrases about particular nouns:

* The horse was panting hard.
* The horse **that won the race** was panting hard.

We use different relative pronouns for different types of nouns:

* **who/whom/whose** can describe *people*
* **that** can describe *people* and *things*
* **which** can describe *things*
In this sentence, we're describing "chocolates", so we should use the relative pronoun **"that"**.

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct relative pronoun!**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:

We use different relative pronouns for different types of nouns:

* **who/whom/whose** can describe *people*

* **which** can describe *things*
In this sentence, we're describing a "tree", so we should use the relative pronoun **"which"**.

khan_exercises.title: Introduction to phrases and clauses
khan_exercises.description: Learn to spot the differences between phrases and clauses.

items.question_content:
**Is this a phrase or a clause?**

> “The calm before the storm”

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
A **phrase** is any group of words that acts like a single part of speech. Phrases don’t have both a subject and a verb:

* _My best friend_—a noun phrase

* _with the blue shirt_—an adjective phrase

* _for twenty days_—an adverb phrase

A **clause** is a group of words that *does* have a subject and a verb:

* *Before I went to the store*—a dependent clause (can't be its own sentence)

 Subject: "I"
 
 Verb: "went"

* *Candy damages your teeth*—an independent clause (*can* be its own sentence)

 Subject: "candy"

 Verb: "damages"
This doesn't contain a verb, so it must be a **phrase**.

options.explanation:
Remember: 

A **phrase** is any group of words that work together, but phrases don't include both a subject and a verb.

A **clause** must contain a subject and a verb.

hints:
A **phrase** is any group of words that acts like a single part of speech. Phrases don’t have both a subject and a verb:

* _My best friend_—a noun phrase

* _with the blue shirt_—an adjective phrase

* _for twenty days_—an adverb phrase

A **clause** is a group of words that *does* have a subject and a verb:

* *Before I went to the store*—a dependent clause (can't be its own sentence)

 Subject: "I"
 
 Verb: "went"

* *Candy damages your teeth*—an independent clause (*can* be its own sentence)

 Subject: "candy"

 Verb: "damages"
The bolded section contains a subject ("Sofia") and a verb ("was wandering"), so it's a **clause**.

options.explanation:
Remember: 

A **phrase** is any group of words that work together, but phrases don't include both a subject and a verb.

A **clause** must contain a subject and a verb.

The bolded section doesn't contain a verb, so it must be a **phrase**.

khan_exercises.title: Dependent and independent clauses
khan_exercises.description: Independent clauses can be sentences. Dependent clauses can't! Let's take a deeper dive into what that looks like in this exercise.

items.question_content:
**Choose the clause that completes the sentence in a grammatical way.**

>Bring your bathing suit


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
There are two main types of clauses.

* **Dependent** clauses *can't* stand alone as complete sentences:

 *Because I was tired*
* **Independent** clauses *can* stand alone as complete sentences:

 *I went to bed*

We can combine clauses to create complete sentences in the following ways:

* Independent clause + comma + coordinating conjunction + independent clause
* Independent clause + semicolon + independent clause
* Independent clause + dependent clause
* Dependent clause + comma + independent clause

The first part of the sentence is an independent clause. We can complete the sentence with a dependent clause:

>**because there's a pool there.**

choices.content: but you want to go swimming.

We can combine clauses to create complete sentences in the following ways:

* Independent clause + comma + coordinating conjunction + independent clause

* Independent clause + dependent clause
* Dependent clause + comma + independent clause

The first part of the sentence is an independent clause. We can complete the sentence with a dependent clause:

choices.content: and then we go white water rafting?

khan_exercises.title: Phrase and clause placement
khan_exercises.description: Choose the best placement for the phrase or clause in the sentences provided!

items.question_content:
**Combine the sentence and clause provided to create the most logical option.**

> Sentence:
> Maria likes to go scuba diving

> Clause:
> When she is on vacation

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can combine **clauses** to create complete sentences in the following ways:

* Independent clause + comma + coordinating conjunction + independent clause
* Independent clause + semicolon + independent clause
* Independent clause + dependent clause
* Dependent clause + comma + independent clause

**Phrases** can appear at the start, at the end, or in the middle of a clause.

**Most importantly, we must combine phrases and clauses in a way that makes the meaning of the sentence clear!**

The most logical combination is

>**When she is on vacation, Maria likes to go scuba diving.**

choices.content: When she is on vacation, Maria likes to go scuba diving.
Maria when she is on vacation likes to go scuba diving.
Maria likes when she is on vacation to go scuba diving.

items.question_content:
**Combine the sentence and clause provided to create the most logical option.**

> Sentence: I had a fun time
> 

> Clause: After it stopped raining
> 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can combine **clauses** to create complete sentences in the following ways:

* Independent clause + comma + coordinating conjunction + independent clause
* Independent clause + semicolon + independent clause
* Independent clause + dependent clause
* Dependent clause + comma + independent clause

**Phrases** can appear at the start, at the end, or in the middle of a clause.

**Most importantly, we must combine phrases and clauses in a way that makes the meaning of the sentence clear!**

The most logical combination is

>**After it stopped raining, I had a fun time.**

choices.content: I after it stopped raining had a fun time.
After it stopped raining, I had a fun time.
I had after it stopped raining a fun time.

The most logical combination is

>**We need to get out of here before dark.**

choices.content: We need to get out before dark of here.
We need before dark to get out of here.
We need to get out of here before dark.

This chair is so that I don't ever want to get up comfortable.

items.question_content:
**Combine the sentence and clause provided to create the most logical option.**

> Sentence: I'm enjoying this yogurt parfait
> 

> Clause: Even though I'm lactose intolerant
> 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can combine **clauses** to create complete sentences in the following ways:

* Independent clause + comma + coordinating conjunction + independent clause
* Independent clause + semicolon + independent clause
* Independent clause + dependent clause
* Dependent clause + comma + independent clause

**Phrases** can appear at the start, at the end, or in the middle of a clause.

**Most importantly, we must combine phrases and clauses in a way that makes the meaning of the sentence clear!**

The most logical combination is

>**I'm enjoying this yogurt parfait even though I'm lactose intolerant.**

choices.content: I'm enjoying even though I'm lactose intolerant this yogurt parfait.
I'm enjoying this yogurt parfait even though I'm lactose intolerant.
I'm enjoying this even though I'm lactose intolerant yogurt parfait.

**Most importantly, we must combine phrases and clauses in a way that makes the meaning of the sentence clear!**

The most logical combination is

choices.content: When you're with family, do you speak Spanish?
Do you speak when you're with family Spanish?
Do when you're with family you speak Spanish?

khan_exercises.title: Relative clauses
khan_exercises.description: We use relative pronouns to connect descriptive phrases to nouns, creating a relative clause. Test your knowledge of how they work!

items.question_content:
**Choose the relative clause in this sentence.**

> At least the bear who ate all my honey offered to buy me more.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Relative clauses** begin with relative pronouns (who, that, which, whose, where, or when). Since they're *clauses*, they also contain a verb phrase.

Relative clauses are usually used to *describe* and *identify* non-specific nouns. In the examples below, the relative clauses are **bolded**:

* the woman **who wore red**
* the place **where I grew up**
* the team **that won the championship**

In this sentence, the relative clause is

>**who ate all my honey**

* the woman **who wore red**
* the place **where I grew up**
* the team **that won the championship**

In this sentence, the relative clause is

>**which I normally have with me**

choices.content: which I normally have with me
